content.text: Support Shift Handoff Procedures

24/7 support operations for GenAI platforms require well-structured shift handoffs. A poor handoff drops context: an incident that was being actively managed becomes 'unknown status' to the incoming team, a customer commitment made by the outgoing shift gets missed, or an elevated system signal being watched falls off the radar. For GenAI platforms specifically, the consequences of dropped context include safety events going unmonitored, model quality regressions being re-diagnosed from scratch by each shift, and enterprise customers missing SLA check-ins.

A handoff procedure is not just a documentation exercise — it is a transfer of situational awareness. The incoming team should be able to operate at full effectiveness within 15 minutes of shift start, not after spending an hour reviewing ticket queues and dashboards from scratch.

attrs.alt: Support shift handoff packet structure and incoming shift checklist

content.text: The Handoff Packet: Structure and Minimum Content

The handoff packet is a written artifact produced by the outgoing shift 30 minutes before shift end. Writing it 30 minutes early (not at the last minute) gives the outgoing shift time to add detail and gives the incoming shift time to read it before the overlap call.

The packet covers six categories. All six are required; an empty section is noted explicitly (e.g., 'Section 3: No elevated signals at shift end') so the incoming team knows the category was checked, not overlooked.
Section 1: Ongoing Incidents
Active incidents are the highest-priority handoff item. For each open incident:

INCIDENT HANDOFF TEMPLATE
==========================
Incident ID:    INC-2024-0847
SEV:            SEV-2
Started:        14:22 UTC (1h 38m ago at time of writing)
Current IC:     [outgoing engineer name] -> handoff to [incoming engineer name]
Bridge:         https://meet.example.com/inc-0847
Slack:          #incident-2024-0847

Status summary (one paragraph):
  The embedding service latency spiked at 14:22 UTC following a config push
  at 14:18. P99 latency is currently 4.2s vs. 0.8s baseline. Throughput is
  unaffected. The config push has been rolled back but latency has not fully
  recovered. ML platform team (Sarah M.) is investigating residual cache state.

Current hypothesis: Cache warm-up after rollback; expect recovery by 15:45 UTC.

Next action:    Check P99 at 15:45 UTC; if not < 1.2s, escalate to infra lead.
Customer comms: Status page updated at 14:35 UTC. 3 enterprise customers notified.
                Next update due: 16:00 UTC (incoming shift owns this).
Open questions: Is the cache invalidation automatic after rollback, or manual?

Every field in this template must be populated. If IC handoff has not been formally agreed, the handoff packet should flag this: 'IC handoff not yet confirmed with incoming — confirm before overlap call ends.'
Section 2: Open L3 Tickets
List all L3 tickets that are open and not yet declared incidents. For each:
Field
Example
Ticket ID
Title
'Output quality degraded for legal summarisation use case'
Customer
Acme Corp (Enterprise)
Age
3h 20m
Assigned L3
[outgoing engineer name]
Investigation state
Reproduced in test env; suspected prompt context truncation; awaiting customer system prompt
Next action + owner
Customer sharing system prompt by 16:30; incoming shift to follow up if not received by 17:00
Watch for incident?
Not yet; if 2 more enterprise customers report same issue before 18:00, consider SEV-2
Section 3: Elevated System Signals
Dashboard signals that are above normal but below incident threshold. The outgoing shift has context on why these are elevated — the incoming shift needs that context to interpret them correctly.

ELEVATED SIGNALS AT SHIFT END
==============================
Signal: Safety filter trigger rate
  Current: 0.18% of requests (baseline: 0.12%)
  Elevated since: 13:05 UTC
  Known cause: Viral prompt pattern circulating on social media (Twitter thread
               about bypassing content filters). L3 aware; no action taken yet.
  Watch threshold: If rate exceeds 0.30%, notify Safety team lead and consider
                   pattern-level mitigation.

Signal: L1 queue depth
  Current: 47 tickets (baseline: 22 at this time of day)
  Elevated since: 14:00 UTC
  Known cause: New feature release this morning generated wave of onboarding
               questions. KB article published at 14:15 but not yet widely seen.
  Expected resolution: Naturally drains as KB article propagates through help
                       center suggestions; no L2 escalation pattern yet.

Signal: None others above baseline at time of writing.
Section 4: Pending Customer Commitments
Any commitment made to a customer that requires action from the incoming shift:

PENDING CUSTOMER COMMITMENTS
==============================
1. Acme Corp (Enterprise) — SEV-2 incident
   Commitment: Status update at 16:00 UTC
   Context: CEO of Acme is personally monitoring; tone must be measured and factual.
   Action: Post update to incident channel AND email account manager (Joe B.)
   Contact: Account manager is Joe B.; his mobile is in the enterprise contacts doc.

2. Meridian Health (Enterprise) — TKT-88112 (L2 quality complaint)
   Commitment: L2 engineer promised to share reproduction steps by 17:00 UTC
   Status: L2 has the steps drafted; need 30 min to finalize and send.
   Action: Incoming L2 (incoming name) pick up TKT-88112 and send by 17:00.

3. Developer survey respondent — TKT-88091 (self-serve, feedback ticket)
   Commitment: Acknowledged and thanked by end of day
   Action: L1 can handle; close with thanks template #47.
Section 5: Active Workarounds
Any workarounds in effect that change how support should handle tickets in specific areas:

ACTIVE WORKAROUNDS
==================
1. Embedding model v2.1 rollback in effect
   Context: v2.1 introduced latency regression (INC-2024-0831, resolved yesterday).
            v2.0 is current production. Customers asking about 'model changelog'
            should be told v2.1 rollout was paused pending investigation.
   Affects: All embedding API calls.
   Expected resolution: v2.1 patched version expected next week.

2. PDF parsing: files > 50MB fail silently
   Context: Infrastructure team aware; fix in next sprint.
            KB article published (KB-0447).
   Workaround: Ask customers to split large PDFs; L1 has runbook.
   Affects: Document ingestion pipeline customers.
   No active incident; do not escalate unless new pattern emerges.
Section 6: Watch Items
Things that are not yet issues but require attention from the incoming shift:
Model version 3.2 deployment scheduled for 22:00 UTC tonight — L3 on-call should be ready for regression reports after deployment; deployment runbook is in #deployments channel
Enterprise customer Orion Ltd has not renewed their SLA contract (expires tomorrow) — account manager Kate L. is handling; if Orion opens a P1 ticket, route to Kate before standard SLA process
Safety team requested all tickets tagged 'jailbreak-attempt' be flagged to them directly — check ticket queue for any tagged since 13:00 and forward
The Overlap Call

The overlap call is a 10-minute live sync between the outgoing and incoming shift at the shift boundary. It is not a status read-out of the handoff packet — the incoming team should have read the packet already. The overlap call serves three purposes:
Confirm understanding of active incidents — incoming IC acknowledges they understand the current state and are taking over
Clarify ambiguities in the packet — anything the incoming team read and could not interpret from context alone
Transfer verbal context that is hard to write — tone of a customer interaction, whether an on-call engineer seems fatigued, a hunch about where an investigation is going

The overlap call should not run long. If it is regularly running to 20+ minutes, the handoff packet is under-specified — context that is being conveyed verbally should be written down. Verbal-only handoffs fail when the outgoing engineer is unavailable for questions after the call.
When There Is an Active Incident at Handoff Time
An active incident at shift boundary requires a different procedure:
Incoming IC joins the incident bridge  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'before', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  the overlap call — do not wait

Outgoing IC formally hands off incident command: ' {'type': 'text', 'text': 'I am handing IC to [incoming name] effective now', 'marks': [{'type': 'italic'}]} ' — this must be stated explicitly on the bridge, not implied
Outgoing engineer stays on the bridge for 30 minutes after formal handoff to answer questions, then drops to standby
The overlap call covers everything else (non-incident items) after the incident handoff is complete

The explicit 'IC handoff to [name]' statement is critical. Incidents have failed at shift boundaries because both engineers assumed the other had IC. A missed handoff leaves the incident without a clear owner exactly when attention is most needed.
Handoff Procedures by Tier
Each support tier has different handoff requirements based on the types of issues they own:
L1 Handoff Procedure

L1 handoffs are primarily about queue state and pending commitments. L1 does not own incidents directly, so the handoff is lower stakes than L2/L3, but missed L1 commitments directly affect customer experience.
L1 handoff item
Required detail
Ticket queue state
Volume above or below normal? Specific spike categories?
Tickets awaiting customer response
List with ticket IDs and deadline for follow-up
Tickets in progress (assigned but open)
Where they are in the resolution process
Runbook gaps encountered
Any new issue types without a runbook — flag for KB review
Proactive status page comms needed
Any customer-visible issues that L1 should acknowledge proactively
L2 Handoff Procedure
L2 owns active technical investigations. These are the items most likely to go cold at shift boundary:
Every open L2 ticket must have a next action and an owner assigned before the outgoing L2 engineer hands off — never leave a ticket in 'investigating' state with no next step
If an investigation is mid-stream (e.g., waiting for a customer to share logs), write the current hypothesis and what the incoming engineer should do when the logs arrive
If an investigation is blocked on L3 (waiting for L3 response), note what was escalated and when L3 acknowledged — the incoming L2 should follow up if L3 has not responded by the time noted
L3 / On-Call Handoff Procedure
L3 on-call handoffs are the highest-stakes because L3 handles active incidents and safety events. In addition to the standard handoff packet:
Outgoing L3 on-call must be reachable for 30 minutes after handoff for questions
Incoming L3 must independently verify dashboard state — do not rely solely on the packet; the outgoing shift may have missed a developing signal
Any pending deployment or scheduled maintenance during the incoming shift is explicitly noted with runbook links
Safety team contact is confirmed to be current — if safety team lead changed, ensure the correct person is in the on-call contact doc
Handoff Quality Metrics
Track handoff quality to detect structural problems in how shifts are transitioning:
Metric
Target
What a failure indicates
Packet completion rate
100% of shifts produce a complete packet
Outgoing shift under time pressure; packet written at last minute
Overlap call duration
8–12 min average
Too short: packet under-specified. Too long: packet missing critical written context
Post-handoff follow-up rate
< 10% of shifts require outgoing engineer after overlap call ends
Incoming team not reading packet, or packet missing key context
Incidents dropped at boundary
Incident handoff was not explicit; IC transfer not confirmed
Customer commitment miss rate
Pending commitments section incomplete or not transferred
Common Handoff Failures for GenAI Support
Failure mode
Why it happens
Fix
Safety signal not noted in packet
Outgoing shift considered it 'not quite elevated enough to mention'
Any signal above baseline must appear in the packet; incoming team decides whether to act
Model version context missing
Outgoing shift knows which version is deployed; forgot to write it down
Include current production model version as a standing item in every packet
Active workaround omitted
Workaround was implemented by a different engineer who didn't write it in the packet
Any workaround must be logged in a shared workarounds doc that feeds the packet automatically
IC handoff not confirmed verbally
Outgoing IC left the call; incoming IC assumed they had it
Require explicit verbal confirmation on the incident bridge; log in incident channel
Packet written at shift end, not 30 min early
Engineer was too busy; wrote it in the last 5 minutes
Enforce 30-min rule via automated reminder; packet quality degrades sharply when written under pressure
Handoff in Globally Distributed Teams
When support teams are distributed across time zones (e.g., Americas, EMEA, APAC), shift handoffs span geographies and cultural communication norms. Additional considerations:
All handoff packets must be written in English as the shared language, regardless of the team's primary language
Time zones must be explicit — write 'UTC' not 'local time' in every timestamp
The overlap call may require one team to be online outside their normal hours; rotate this burden equitably across teams

When a major model deployment happens during APAC hours (which is outside business hours for Americas/EMEA), APAC must own the initial response and produce a detailed handoff packet for the next shift
Regional public holidays create staffing gaps — pre-plan coverage a week in advance and note in the handoff packet when the incoming team is reduced-staffing
Automating Handoff Packet Generation

Manual handoff packets are error-prone under time pressure. Engineers at shift end are often dealing with active issues; writing a thorough packet competes with resolving the last ticket or completing an investigation. Partial automation reduces this tension.
What Can Be Auto-Generated
The ticketing system and observability platform hold most of the data needed for the handoff packet. An automated handoff generator can pre-populate:
Packet section
Data source
Automation approach
Ongoing incidents
Incident management system (PagerDuty, Opsgenie)
Pull all open incidents with SEV, IC, duration, and last update; insert into packet template
Open L3 tickets
Ticketing system (Zendesk, Jira) filtered by tier=L3, status=open
Pull ticket ID, title, customer, age, assigned engineer, last note; insert into table
Elevated signals
Observability platform (Datadog, Grafana) alert state
Query current alert state; mark anything in WARN or CRITICAL state
Pending commitments
Ticketing system — tickets with 'follow-up-due' tag and due date
Pull all tickets with due dates in next 12 hours; sort by priority
Active workarounds
Shared workarounds doc (Confluence, Notion)
Pull all workarounds with status=active; include expiry if set

What cannot be auto-generated: the narrative status for ongoing incidents, the hypothesis and next action for L3 investigations, and the watch items section. These require human judgment and must be written by the outgoing engineer. The auto-generation reduces the packet from 'write everything from scratch' to 'review the pre-populated data and add narrative context.'
Example Auto-Generated Packet Draft

SHIFT HANDOFF PACKET — AUTO-GENERATED DRAFT
Shift: Shift A | Date: 2024-11-15 | Generated: 15:30 UTC
Outgoing engineer: [FILL IN NAME]

=== SECTION 1: ONGOING INCIDENTS ===
[AUTO] INC-2024-0847 | SEV-2 | Started: 14:22 UTC (1h 8m ago)
  IC: [FILL IN IC NAME]
  Last update: 15:18 UTC
  *** ENGINEER: Add current status summary and next action ***

=== SECTION 2: OPEN L3 TICKETS ===
[AUTO] TKT-88234 | Acme Corp (Enterprise) | Age: 3h 20m | Assigned: [outgoing name]
  Title: Output quality degraded for legal summarisation
  Last note: 14:45 UTC
  *** ENGINEER: Add investigation state and next action ***

[AUTO] TKT-88112 | Meridian Health (Enterprise) | Age: 1h 05m | Assigned: [outgoing name]
  Title: Embedding latency spike for large document batches
  Last note: 15:02 UTC
  *** ENGINEER: Add investigation state and next action ***

=== SECTION 3: ELEVATED SIGNALS ===
[AUTO] ALERT: safety-filter-trigger-rate | Current: WARN (0.18%, baseline 0.12%)
  *** ENGINEER: Add context on why this is elevated ***
[AUTO] ALERT: l1-queue-depth | Current: CLEAR (resolved 15:15 UTC)

=== SECTION 4: PENDING COMMITMENTS ===
[AUTO] TKT-88234 follow-up due: 16:00 UTC | Customer: Acme Corp | Priority: Enterprise
  *** ENGINEER: Add commitment details and context ***

=== SECTION 5: ACTIVE WORKAROUNDS ===
[AUTO] Embedding model v2.0 (rollback from v2.1) | Status: active | No expiry set
[AUTO] PDF parsing > 50MB silent failure | Status: active | Fix expected: next sprint

=== SECTION 6: WATCH ITEMS ===
*** ENGINEER: Add manually — no auto-generation available ***

The outgoing engineer fills in the  {'type': 'text', 'text': '*** ENGINEER: ***', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  sections and deletes any auto-generated items that need correction. This reduces packet writing time from 20-30 minutes to 5-10 minutes for standard shifts.
Handoff During Degraded Staffing
On-call weekends, public holidays, and reduced-staffing periods require different handoff norms because the incoming team may have fewer engineers available to absorb context.
Reduced-Staffing Handoff Considerations
Packet section 6 (watch items) becomes more important — items that would normally 'watch themselves' need explicit monitoring instructions because there are fewer engineers watching
The overlap call should be extended to 20 minutes to allow more thorough verbal handoff

Any L3 issue that was previously 'watching, not yet escalated' should be promoted to an incident if the incoming team is reduced-staffing — better to have an active incident with a defined owner than an L3 issue that requires judgment calls from a stretched team
Outgoing engineer availability after handoff should be extended from 30 minutes to 2 hours for critical holiday coverage periods
Inform enterprise account managers when their customers' SLA coverage window will be supported by reduced staffing, so customers have context if response times are slower
Model Deployment Handoffs
Model deployments are a GenAI-specific handoff scenario that creates temporary elevated risk. A model deployment that happens near a shift boundary requires:

MODEL DEPLOYMENT HANDOFF CHECKLIST
====================================
Pre-deployment (before shift end):
  [ ] Deployment scheduled time confirmed with ML platform team
  [ ] Deployment runbook linked in handoff packet Section 6
  [ ] Rollback procedure documented and accessible to incoming L3
  [ ] Outgoing L3 availability confirmed: available on pager for 2h after handoff
  [ ] Enterprise customers notified per deployment communication plan

At deployment time (incoming shift):
  [ ] L3 on-call monitoring error rates from T+0 (deployment complete)
  [ ] Quality eval running against new model version (if automated)
  [ ] L2 informed to watch for quality complaints spike in ticket queue
  [ ] Support team briefed on behavioral differences in new version (if any)

If regression detected after deployment:
  [ ] Declare SEV-2 immediately (do not wait for ticket volume to confirm)
  [ ] Page outgoing L3 (they are on standby for exactly this)
  [ ] Initiate rollback per runbook (rollback authority: ML platform team)
  [ ] Update status page: "Investigation in progress following model update"
Summary

Support shift handoff procedures for GenAI platforms require structure and discipline because the failure modes are more consequential than in traditional software support. A dropped incident context means a safety event goes unmonitored. A missed model deployment note means the incoming shift is surprised by a regression. A missing customer commitment means an enterprise customer misses an SLA check-in. The handoff packet — covering open incidents, open L3 tickets, elevated signals, pending commitments, active workarounds, and watch items — is written 30 minutes before shift end and is non-negotiable in completeness. The overlap call confirms understanding and transfers verbal context; it assumes the packet has been read. The incident IC handoff at shift boundary is always explicit and verbal. Handoff quality is tracked through packet completion rate, overlap call duration, post-handoff follow-up rate, and incidents dropped at boundary.
Key takeaways:
The handoff packet is written 30 minutes before shift end, not at the last minute
All six packet sections are required; empty sections are noted explicitly to confirm they were checked
When an active incident exists at shift boundary, the incoming IC joins the bridge before the overlap call and IC transfer is stated explicitly
Every open L2 investigation must have a next action and assigned owner before handoff
Track handoff quality: packet completion rate, overlap duration, post-handoff follow-ups, and incidents dropped at boundary
Global teams: all timestamps in UTC; overlap call burden rotated equitably across regions
